coredata.dc:title: Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots with red fluorescence: An optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging sensor for identifying Chinese Baijiu quality
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zihan
Liu, Yang
Liang, Meiqi
Chen, Yihong
Dong, Wenjuan
Hu, Qin
Song, Shengmei
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Chinese Baijiu (Liquor) is a popular alcoholic beverage, and the ethanol content in Baijiu is closely related to its quality; therefore, it is of great significance to explore a facile, sensitive, and rapid method to detect ethanol content in Baijiu. Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (H-CQDs) with bright red fluorescence (24.14 %) were fabricated by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine, p-aminobenzoic acid, manganese chloride, and hydrochloric acid as reaction precursors. After the introduction of ultrapure water into the ethanol solution dissolved with H-CQDs, the aggregated H-CQDs resulted in significant changes in fluorescence intensity and absorbance. On this basis, a sensor for detecting ethanol by optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging was constructed. More importantly, the sensor can be used for detecting ethanol content in Chinese Baijiu with satisfactory results. This sensing platform has great potential for quality identification in Chinese Baijiu, broadening the application scope of CQDs in food safety detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots
Chinese Baijiu
Ethanol
Optical dual-mode
Smartphone imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ethanol content by H-CQDs
Optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of ethanol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development ( YDZJSX2021A004 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21705101 and 21874087 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) diameter distribution, (C) EDXA spectrum, and (D) element mapping of H-CQDs.
(A) XPS survey scan, (B) Cl 2p XPS, (C) Mn 2p XPS, and (D) FTIR spectrum of H-CQDs.

(A) PL excitation (pink line), PL emission (pink line), and UV–vis absorption (purple line) spectra of H-CQDs (5.0 μg mL−1). Inset: the photographs of H-CQDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right) irradiation. (B) PL emission behavior of H-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) PL and (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of H-CQDs (5.0 μg mL−1) at different ethanol contents under λ ex of 576 nm. Plot of (B) fluorescence intensity and (D) absorbance of H-CQDs versus ethanol content. Linearity between R/R0 obtained in (E) visible light and (F) UV light and ethanol content. R0 and R are the R values of H-CQDs smartphone imaging in anhydrous ethanol without and with water, respectively. Inset: The photograms of H-CQDs (5.0 μg mL−1) dissolved in anhydrous ethanol containing different water under (E) visible light and (F) UV light irradiation.

(A) Zeta potentials and (B) DLS histogram of H-CQDs in various ultrapure water and ethanol mixtures. (C) The fluorescence decay curves of H-CQDs (5.0 μg mL−1) in ethanol and water mixtures with ethanol contents of 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, 60 %, and 80 %. (D) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence mechanism of ethanol detection by H-CQDs.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process and ethanol detection of H-CQDs.
Comparison of the constructed sensor based on H-CQDs and other reported sensors for ethanol detection.

row.entry: Fenyangwang liquor, Niulanshan erguotou, Jingdu erguotou

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CuNCs-CDs: a combination of copper nanoclusters and carbon dots; Au/NaGdF4: Yb3+, Er3+: Au/NaGdF4: 18 % Yb3+, 2 % Er3+ hybrid fluorescent systems; bisNAP: a bisnaphthalimide-based sensor; Si NPs/G-Cu NCs: a combination of silicon nanoparticles and green emitting copper nanoclusters; MF: melamine foam; AuNPs@CDs: gold nanoparticles@carbon dots; H-CQDs: hydrophobic carbon quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Analysis results of H-CQDs-based fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for ethanol content in Chinese Baijiu (n = 3).
(b) Analysis results of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform by H-CQDs for ethanol content in Chinese Baijiu (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots with red fluorescence: An optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging sensor for identifying Chinese Baijiu quality

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Chemistry and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Chemistry and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Chemistry and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chinese Baijiu (Liquor) is a popular alcoholic beverage, and the ethanol content in Baijiu is closely related to its quality; therefore, it is of great significance to explore a facile, sensitive, and rapid method to detect ethanol content in Baijiu. Hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (H-CQDs) with bright red fluorescence (24.14 %) were fabricated by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine, p-aminobenzoic acid, manganese chloride, and hydrochloric acid as reaction precursors. After the introduction of ultrapure water into the ethanol solution dissolved with H-CQDs, the aggregated H-CQDs resulted in significant changes in fluorescence intensity and absorbance. On this basis, a sensor for detecting ethanol by optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging was constructed. More importantly, the sensor can be used for detecting ethanol content in Chinese Baijiu with satisfactory results. This sensing platform has great potential for quality identification in Chinese Baijiu, broadening the application scope of CQDs in food safety detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chinese Baijiu, a traditionally indigenous alcoholic drink, is obtained from the fermentation and distillation of grains and is popular throughout the world [1,2]. Ethanol and water account for more than 99 % of Baijiu, while the remaining components make up about 1 %, including polyols, methanol, aldehydes, acids, esters, etc [3,4]. Different flavors, types, and brands of Chinese Baijiu are popular in the market, so reliable ethanol detection methods are required to regulate the market. In the process of industrial production, density gravimetry and specific gravity meters are commonly used to detect ethanol content, but both require big sample volumes [5]. Other methods for detecting ethanol, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], infrared spectroscopy [2], colorimetric method [7], gas chromatography (GC) [8], etc., are costly and time-consuming, which are difficult to meet the requirements for rapid on-site detection. Fortunately, some nanomaterials have been used to design some fluorescent probes/sensors for detecting ethanol content. Li et al. [5] combined silicon nanoparticles with green luminescent copper nanoclusters (Si NPs/G-CuNCs) to construct a visual dual-emission fluorescence probe and used it for ethanol detection. Consequently, it is important and urgent to establish a straightforward and easy-to-use sensor for the determination of ethanol.

Single-readout sensors with simple pre-processing, good selectivity, and high sensitivity have been widely used to detect trace substances [9,10]. However, single-readout nanosensors with a sole signal output channel may make the detection results unreliable or inaccurate when detecting some complex real samples. To ensure the accuracy of the detection results, dual-readout nanosensors, especially colorimetric and fluorometric dual-readout nanosensors, have been widely concerned and rapidly developed [11]. Tantawy et al. [12] constructed a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode nanosensor based on gold nanoparticles and fluorescent carbon dots system (AuNPs/CDs) and used it for the determination of ketamine hydrochloride (KET) in soda drinks. And a dual-readout sensor based on red luminescent carbon dots (r-CDs) was built to detect NO2 − in meat products [13]. Therefore, it is feasible and noteworthy to construct dual-mode sensors. Compared with single-readout sensors, the detection results of dual-readout sensors are more convincing [14], while the application of dual-readout sensors in the field of ethanol detection has not been thoroughly investigated. Likewise, similar to optical dual-readout sensors, the smartphone imaging data under ultraviolet and visible light is more reliable than imaging data under a single light source [15]. Therefore, it is necessary to detect ethanol using optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging sensors constructed from novel nanomaterials, which has important theoretical significance and practical value.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are distinguished nanomaterials that are extensively adopted in fluorescent sensors [10,16] because of their photoluminescence stability, ease of functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, etc [17,18]. Heteroatoms doping has been a preferred method to improve the chemical composition and structural properties of CQDs [19], and metal atom doping confers more excellent properties of CQDs [20,21]. However, the application of CQDs for ethanol detection is still very limited [22], especially for metal-doped CQDs. The reported fluorescent sensors constructed based on CQDs must rely on another nanomaterial for the detection of ethanol [22], increasing the complexity of the constructed sensors. Hence, we want to prepare metal-doped CQDs for developing a simple, convenient, and ready-to-use sensor for fast and sensitive ethanol detection.

In this work, hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (H-CQDs) with red fluorescence, which were successfully doped with manganese, chlorine, and nitrogen, were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine (OPD), p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), manganese chloride (MnCl2), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) (Scheme 1 ). Based on H-CQDs, a dual-mode optical sensor was constructed for the fast and accurate determination of ethanol. The visual and quantitative detection of ethanol was also performed using a dual-mode smartphone imaging colorimetric method. Compared with most CQDs-based ethanol detection sensors, the proposed method have obvious advantages: (1) the sensor is straightforward and can be constructed without the assistance of other materials; (2) improved reliability of ethanol detection results by introducing multi-signal outputs and intelligent recognition; (3) the incorporation of a smartphone into the sensor system makes quantitative detection of ethanol easier to achieve than some sensors that utilize visual recognition for semi-quantitative detection of ethanol. The excellent performance of the constructed sensor in detecting real samples fully illustrates the enormous potential of H-CQDs in determining and monitoring ethanol content in Chinese Baijiu.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD), p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), manganese chloride (MnCl2), H2O2, potassium chloride (KCl), and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) were acquired from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (EtOH, ≥ 99.7 %), chloroform (CF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethyl acetate (EA), ethylene glycol (EG), acetonitrile (ACN), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and methanol (MeOH) were bought from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). All chemicals are AR (Analytical Reagent) grade and can be used directly without treatment. Ultrapure water (≥ 18.25 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experiments, which was obtained from a Molecular Ultrapure Water System (Shanghai, China).

0.0540 g OPD, 0.0685 g PABA, 0.0629 g MnCl2, and 150 μL concentrated HCl (36.5 %) were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. The above solution was treated in an autoclave reactor for 8 h at 200 °C. The crude solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to obtain precipitation. The precipitate was washed with ultrapure water to acquire pure precipitation products. After dissolving the above precipitate in ethanol, it was filtered through 0.22 μm organic filtration membrane and then subjected to spin evaporation. Finally, solid powders of H-CQDs were obtained by the freeze-drying process.

H-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, optical contact angle instrument, fluorescence spectrum, fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and zeta potential. The instrument details and specific parameters can be found in our previously published paper [15].

The fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of H-CQDs was calculated by the equation [15]:
Φ X
=
Φ
S T
(
G r a d
X
G r a d

)
(
η X 2
η

2
)
where Grad is the gradient of the linear relationship between the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance. The subscripts "X" and "ST" denote the sample H-CQDs and the standard cresol violet, respectively. The refractive index is expressed as η. The FQY of H-CQDs in ethanol at excitation and absorption wavelengths of 576 nm was calculated using cresol violet dissolved in ethanol (FQY = 0.51) as the standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ethanol content by H-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of H-CQDs (5.0 μg mL−1) in various ethanol-ultrapure water solutions were measured. An optical dual-mode sensor was developed to detect ethanol, utilizing the PL intensities at 654 nm, as well as the absorbance ratio (A285 nm/A630 nm). To ensure the stability of the light source during image capture, we choose to simulate ultraviolet (UV) light and visible light in a darkroom using a 365 nm UV lamp and a white light bulb, respectively. Images were captured at fixed angles and positions to ensure image quality and minimize background interference. Under preset UV and visible light exposure conditions, the colors of the reaction solutions were recorded using the camera of a smartphone, and image analysis was performed using Color Identification software on the smartphone.

Acetone (AC), chloroform (CF), DMF, EA, EG, ACN, DMSO, or MeOH were mixed with ethanol to ensure that the ethanol content and other solvent content were 95 % and 5 %, respectively, in order to explore the interfering effects of various solvents on the determination of ethanol by H-CQDs (5.0 μg mL−1). Changes in the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the above solutions were assessed in detail, and the color information of the captured images was analyzed using a smartphone. All measurements were taken in triplicate to assure the credibility of the results.

Three commercial Chinese Baijiu were purchased from the local market including Fenyangwang liquor (Shanxi Fenyangwang Wine Co., Ltd., Shanxi, China), Niulanshan erguotou (Beijing Shunxin Agriculture Co., Ltd., Beijing, China), and Jingdu erguotou (Beijing Royal Jingdu Wine Co., Ltd., Beijing, China), which were labeled with ethanol content of 39 %, 46 %, and 56 %, respectively. Then, 2.0 mL of different Chinese Baijiu sample solutions were mixed with H-CQDs (5.0 μg mL−1), rspectively, and the ethanol content was detected according to the method in “2.5 Detection of ethanol content by H-CQDs”. Fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and images under visible light and UV light irradiation of the mixed solutions were acquired in detail to analyze the ethanol content in the actual samples and their spiked recoveries.

H-CQDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using reaction precursors including OPD, PABA, MnCl2, and HCl. To achieve the optimal H-CQDs, extensive investigations were conducted on the ratio of precursors and reaction time (Fig. S1). It can be seen that the molar ratios of OPD to PABA (Fig. S1A) and OPD to MnCl2 (Fig. S1B) were both 1:1. The concentration of HCl (Fig. S1C), reaction temperature, and time (Fig. S1D) were 180 mM, 200 °C, and 8 h, respectively. H-CQDs synthesized using optimum parameters had the highest FQY in ethanol and were used in all subsequent experiments.

The size and morphology of H-CQDs were assessed using TEM and HRTEM. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, H-CQDs are uniformly dispersed on the copper grid, exhibiting a quasi-spherical structure. The lattice spacing of H-CQDs is 0.28 nm, corresponding to (020) plane of graphitic carbon [23]. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the average particle size of H-CQDs is 2.85 ± 0.2 nm, and the particle size distribution ranged from 1.36 nm to 3.04 nm. Furthermore, the EDXA spectrum (Fig. 1C) and element mapping (Fig. 1D) were used to investigate the elemental composition of H-CQDs, displaying that H-CQDs are mainly composed of Mn, Cl, O, N, and C.

To explore the chemical composition and surface functional groups of H-CQDs, XPS and FTIR were investigated in detail. In Fig. 2 A, the XPS survey scan of H-CQDs reveals five characteristic peaks at 195.0, 282.0, 396.0, 529.0, and 639.0 eV, which correspond to Cl 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Mn 2p, respectively. The results of integral area and rate sensitivity factors (RSF) indicated that the contents of Mn, Cl, O, N, and C were 0.62 %, 1.41 %, 15.56 %, 23.56 %, and 58.84 %, respectively. The C 1s XPS spectrum of H-CQDs (Fig. S2A) reveals five peaks at 284.3, 284.6, 285.1, 285.7, and 286.2 eV, which associated with C–C, C–Mn, C–O, C–N/CN, and C–Cl, respectively [11,24]. In the N 1s XPS spectrum of H-CQDs (Fig. S2B), graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and amino N were clearly present at 400.2, 399.4, and 398.4 eV, respectively [15]. The O 1s XPS spectrum of H-CQDs (Fig. S2C) exhibits three peaks at 533.7, 532.5, and 531.5 eV, attributing to O–Cl, OC, and O–C, respectively [11,24]. As depicted in Fig. 2B, it is evident that the Cl 2p XPS spectrum of H-CQDs shows four distinct peaks at 199.5 (Cl–N), 198.5 (Cl–C), 197.7 (Cl–O), and 197.1 eV (Cl–H) [25]. Similarly, the Mn 2p XPS spectrum of H-CQDs (Fig. 2C) displays three peaks at 641.2, 642.5, and 646.3 eV, which represent Mn 2p3/2, Mn–O, and Mn–C, respectively [24,26]. Moreover, the FTIR was utilized to identify the functional groups present in H-CQDs and to verify its chemical composition (Fig. 2D). C–O, CO, and O–H/N–H correspond to distinct peaks at 1221, 1620, and 3415 cm−1, respectively [11,13]. Nitrogen-containing peaks at 1523, 1141, and 747 cm−1 belong to νC
N, νC-N, and νN-H, respectively [11,13,25]. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum of H-CQDs reveals the presence of νMn-O (599 cm−1) and νC-Cl (484 cm−1), highlighting the existence of Mn and Cl in H-CQDs [11,25]. Generally, hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CQDs mainly include oxygen-containing substances such as C–O, CO, and O–C–O, while hydrophobic functional groups are mainly C–C and C–H [27,28]. From the results of XPS and FTIR, it can be observed that N, Mn, and Cl have been successfully doped into H-CQDs, resulting in the presence of relatively abundant hydrophobic functional groups on the surface of H-CQDs, including C–C and C–H. Finally, the wettability of H-CQDs was evaluated through contact angle (CA) measurements. Typically, a solid surface with a CA value exceeding 90° is considered hydrophobic [29]. The CA of H-CQDs is determined to be 93.1° (Fig. S2D), indicating that the surface of H-CQDs is hydrophobic. Combining the results from FTIR, XPS, and CA, it is evident that compared to the hydrophilic functional groups in H-CQDs, the content of hydrophobic functional groups is more abundant. This endows H-CQDs with excellent hydrophobicity, making them suitable for constructing sensors with specific functionalities.

Considering the doping characteristics and unique structure of H-CQDs, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy were used to investigate their optical features. The distinct peak is observed at 283 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of H-CQDs (purple line in Fig. 3 A), which may be due to the π→π* transition of CC [30]. Also, two broad peaks at 532 nm and 565 nm represent n→π* transitions of surface groups containing C–O and CN/CO structures [30]. H-CQDs exhibit excellent solubility in ethanol, forming a transparent pink solution. While under UV lamp irradiation, H-CQDs can display intense red fluorescence (inset in Fig. 3A). The optimal emission and excitation peaks of H-CQDs are located at 654 nm and 576 nm, respectively. The PL emission spectra of H-CQDs in ethanol at different excitation wavelengths did not show any change (Fig. 3B), revealing an excitation-independent PL feature. Using cresol violet as the standard reference (Fig. S3A), the relative FQY of H-CQDs in ethanol was computed to be 24.14 % (Fig. S3B). The outstanding luminescence properties of H-CQDs make them a great potential for fluorescence sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical dual-mode and smartphone imaging detection of ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: The most meaningful application of H-CQDs in optical sensing is the highly sensitive detection of ethanol. H-CQDs were added to mixtures of water and ethanol in different ratios, and the sensitivity of the sensor was evaluated by measuring the PL intensities at 654 nm and the absorbances at 285 nm and 630 nm. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, the PL intensity of H-CQDs increased as the water content in anhydrous ethanol increased from 15.8 % to 80.0 %. In Fig. 4B, the Stern–Volmer (SV) plot displays F/F0 against the concentration of ethanol, where F and F0 represent the PL intensities of H-CQDs in different ethanol and water mixtures and anhydrous ethanol, respectively. In the range of 15.8–80.0 %, a significant linear relationship can be found between the ethanol content and F/F0. The linear equation, R2, and LOD were F/F0 = 0.0131c(ethanol) + 0.3522, 0.9932, and 0.14 %, respectively, where c represents the ethanol content. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 4C, the absorbance of H-CQDs at 285 nm gradually increased with the increase of ethanol content, while the absorbance at 630 nm gradually decreased. A285 nm/A630 nm and ethanol content demonstrated excellent linearity in the range of 25.7–84.9 % with linear equation, R2, and LOD of A285 nm/A630 nm = 0.2868c(ethanol) – 3.7847, 0.9934, and 0.05 %, respectively. A285 nm and A630 nm are the absorbances of H-CQDs in different water and ethanol mixtures at 285 nm and 630 nm, respectively, while c is the content of ethanol (Fig. 4D). The above data demonstrate that H-CQDs can be used as optical dual-mode sensors for detecting ethanol concentration, with the advantages of low detection limit, wide linear range, and simple operation.

Interestingly, the color of H-CQDs dissolved in a water and ethanol mixture exhibited significant changes under the irradiation of visible light (Fig. 4E) and UV light (Fig. 4F). Hence, a smartphone-based colorimetric method was developed for real-time monitoring of ethanol contents. Smartphone imaging colorimetry offers distinct advantages and higher sensitivity in detecting color changes compared to visual inspection with the naked eye. Smartphone cameras can quickly and accurately capture several images under visible and UV light exposure. Subsequently, the smartphone's Color Identification software accurately recognizes the colors in an image and convert them to R, G, and B values. Finally, the linear relationship between ethanol content and RGB values was calculated using WPS Office software. A linear relationship was established by plotting R/R0 against ethanol content (Fig. 4E and F). R0 is the R value obtained by converting the image of H-CQDs dissolved in anhydrous ethanol under visible light or UV light irradiation. R is the R value obtained by converting the images of H-CQDs dissolved in a solvent mixture of anhydrous ethanol and water under visible light or UV light irradiation. Utilizing R/R0 can effectively reduce the influence of external factors on the image acquisition procedure. As depicted in Fig. 4E, the color of H-CQDs varied from purple to pink with increasing ethanol content under visible light irradiation, and the fitted linear equation, linear range, R2, and LOD for R/R0 versus ethanol content were R/R0 = 0.0022 c(ethanol) + 0.7764, 10.9–99.7 %, 0.9937, and 1.18 %, respectively. Similarly, the fluorescence color of H-CQDs changed from dark red to bright red with increasing ethanol content under UV irradiation (Fig. 4F). Ethanol content and R/R0 depicted a good linear relationship in the range of 35.5–65.2 % with a linear regression equation, R2, and LOD of R/R0 = 0.0088 c(ethanol) + 0.1737, 0.9948, and 0.93 %, respectively (Fig. 4F). These results provide comprehensive evidence of the applicability of a smartphone-assisted visible and fluorescence dual-mode imaging system for fast, sensitive, immediate, and portable quantitative detection of ethanol content within a wide range.

To guarantee the accuracy and dependability of the sensor results during the detection process, the fluorescence intensity, absorbance, and R-value (fluorescence imaging and visible imaging) of H-CQDs under salt solution, H2O2, pH, and UV irradiation were studied. Ultra-high concentrations of KCl solution (1.0 M, Fig. S4A), H2O2 (1.0 M, Fig. S4B), pH (4.0–9.0, Fig. S4C), and UV irradiation (180 min, Fig. S4D) had no significant effects on the PL intensity, absorbance, and R-value (fluorescence imaging and visible imaging) of H-CQDs. This indicates that H-CQDs not only exhibit excellent optical stability but also can remain stable in the detection of ethanol concentration in Chinese Baijiu, thus providing assurance for the accurate detection of real samples. The anti-interference performance of H-CQDs in detecting ethanol was extensively investigated by introducing various solvents (CF, DMF, EA, EG, ACN, DMSO, and MeOH). It was observed that all solvents had no impact on the PL intensity (Fig. S5A), absorbance (Fig. S5B), and R/R0 of fluorescence images (Fig. S5C) and visible light images (Fig. S5D) of H-CQDs. This exceptional anti-interference performance of H-CQDs demonstrates that it can accurately and reliably detect ethanol contents in real samples without being affected by impurities. Table 1 provides a comparison between the H-CQDs-based sensor and other published analysis methods for ethanol detection [5,22,31–34], demonstrating that our method performs well in terms of detection limit, application scope, and linear range. In addition, our proposed smartphone imaging colorimetric sensor has not been found in other methods, which can greatly improve the convenience, simplicity, efficiency, and visibility of ethanol detection, and is of great significance for detecting ethanol in real samples.

The PL intensity of H-CQDs gradually decreased with increasing water content in anhydrous ethanol, which may be attributed to the following factors. First, H-CQDs are hydrophobic, i.e., their solubility in ethanol is much greater than their solubility in ultrapure water, resulting in poor dispersibility of H-CQDs in water, and they are highly susceptible to aggregation effects. We hypothesized that with the addition of ultrapure water in anhydrous ethanol, H-CQDs begin to aggregate due to resonance energy transfer and π-π interactions [35,36], resulting in fluorescence quenching of H-CQDs. Second, solvent polarity affects the aggregation of H-CQDs [37]. As the polarity of the solvent increases, the hydrogen-bonding interactions gradually increase, causing the energy gap of the sp2-hybrid core to decrease [37]. The nitrogen- and oxygen-rich functional groups on the H-CQDs surface can increase the carrier density and induce electronic charge transfer to the edges due to the strong influence of the solvent dipole moment [38,39], resulting in the aggregation of H-CQDs. Third, the zeta potential of H-CQDs gradually decreased as the ultrapure water content in ethanol increases (Fig. 5 A), indicating that the weakening of electrostatic repulsion also drives the aggregation of H-CQDs [40]. In addition, the DLS results fully demonstrate the aggregation of H-CQDs with increasing water content in ethanol. As shown in Fig. 5B, the hydrodynamic particle sizes of H-CQDs in ethanol with 0 %, 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % ultrapure water were 85.77 nm, 134.20 nm, 172.50 nm, 203.10 nm, and 221.60 nm, respectively, the progressively increasing hydrated particle size fully proves the aggregation of H-CQDs. The quenching process was investigated using fluorescence lifetimes and the corresponding results are summarized in Fig. 5C and Table S1. The average lifetimes of H-CQDs were 2.20 ns, 1.91 ns, 1.70 ns, 1.63 ns, and 1.22 ns when the contents of ultrapure water in ethanol were 20 %, 40 %, 60 %, 80 %, and 100 %, respectively. The decreasing fluorescence lifetimes indicated that the PL quenching of H-CQDs in ultrapure water is a dynamic process. Overall, we suggest that the hydrophobicity of H-CQDs, increased solvent polarity, and enhanced electrostatic repulsion combine to cause the aggregation effect of H-CQDs in ultrapure water, resulting in dynamic quenching of PL of H-CQDs in ultrapure water (Fig. 5D).

In order to validate the practicality of the constructed sensor for the detection of ethanol in real samples, we performed ethanol detection and recovery experiments using three different Chinese Baijiu, including Fenyangwang liquor, Niulanshan erguotou, and Jingdu erguotou. Table 2 a presents the results of the H-CQDs-based fluorometirc and colorimetric methods for the detection and spiked recovery of ethanol in the selected Chinese Baijiu. The ethanol contents in Fenyangwang liquor, Niulanshan Erguotou, and Jingdu Erguotou were detected by fluorimetry, colorimetry, smartphone-assisted visible-light imaging, and smartphone-assisted UV imaging, respectively. The results were comparable to the factory alcohol values of the three types of Chinese Baijiu (Tables 2a and 2b), indicating that the quality of the detected Chinese Baijiu is excellent. When different concentrations of ethanol (5 % and 10 %) were added to the actual samples, the recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) by fluorimetry, colorimetry, smartphone-assisted visible-light imaging, and smartphone-assisted UV imaging were 98.1–102.0 % and 1.0–5.1 %, 96.7–104.5 % and 1.1–5.2 %, 98.9–103.1 % and 2.1–6.6 %, and 97.7–104.3 % and 1.1–5.9 %, respectively. The findings indicate that the H-CQDs-based optical dual-mode sensor can be utilized for the quantitative analysis of ethanol concentration in Chinese Baijiu samples with simple operation and high accuracy. It is undeniable that the detection results of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform differ from those of the proportional optical sensors due to the fact that the smartphone-assisted sensing platform will inevitably be interfered with by the test environment during the detection process, which will lead to fluctuations in the detection results [41]. Nevertheless, the as-constructed smartphone sensing platform can already meet the demand for ethanol detection in actual samples. However, if the widespread problem of low detection sensitivity prevalent in smartphone-assisted sensing platforms can be solved, it will contribute to the fast growth of smartphone sensing platforms and facilitate the widespread adoption and utilization of smartphone sensors.

In summary, Mn, Cl, and N co-doped H-CQDs prepared by hydrothermal treatment of OPD, PABA, MnCl2, and HCl with excellent luminescence, good photostability, and specific hydrophobicity were successfully used to construct an optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted sensor for detecting ethanol. The as-constructed sensor has good sensitivity (0.14 % for fluorometry, 0.05 % for colorimetry, 1.18 % for smartphone-assisted visible-light imaging, and 0.93 % for smartphone-assisted UV imaging), wide detection range (15.8–80.0 % for fluorometry, 25.7–84.9 % for colorimetry, 10.9–99.7 % for smartphone-assisted visible-light imaging, and 35.5–65.2 % for smartphone-assisted UV imaging), and excellent anti-interference performance. Meanwhile, the sensor has high recovery rates (basically between 96.5 % and 104.5 %) and outstanding reproducibility (RSDs less than 6.6 %), confirming its feasibility in practical sample detection. The rapid, sensitive, and portable method developed in this study performs excellently in Baijiu and has a promising application in the field of Baijiu quality monitoring.

Zihan Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yang Liu: Software. Meiqi Liang: Methodology. Yihong Chen: Validation. Wenjuan Dong: Validation. Qin Hu: Methodology. Shengmei Song: Investigation. Shaomin Shuang: Conceptualization. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development, China (YDZJSX2021A004) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705101 and 21874087).
